TextENGNew: ...............The ceiling... slowly swam into focus.
...It was hot.
...A thin film... of clammy sweat covered my entire body.
...The cicadas' cries seeping into my room... were somehow making my head throb.
changeReason: Old says 'grating'; new says 'making head throb'; Japanese きしませる means to throb/creak.

TextENGNew: Remembering that today was a weekday... my mind snapped to attention.
A moment later, I bolted upright.
The clock read... just before 10.@k...I was completely late.
...If I dragged myself downstairs, Mom would chew me out.@k...But there was nothing I could do about it.
I glanced over the day's schedule and haphazardly repacked my bag.
changeReason: Old says 'suitably rearranged'; new says 'haphazardly repacked'; Japanese 適当に means roughly/haphazardly.

TextENGNew: I threw on my clothes and hurried downstairs.
There was no sign of my parents at all.
...Maybe the two of them had gone out somewhere.
In other words, here's what happened.
...Mom had probably tried to wake me up once this morning, but I'd fallen back asleep without any memory of it.
...My parents, assuming I had already left for school, went out themselves.@k...That was most likely what happened.
...I went to check the front door, and sure enough, it was locked.
Looked like I was right.@kMy deduction was confirmed.
Once I realized my parents were gone, the urgency to rush to school suddenly faded.
A single portion of breakfast had been left in the dining room.
...It was probably mine.
...The glass of milk had long since gone lukewarm.
...This meal—the kind I couldn't even figure out how to provide for Satoko.@k...Last night, I had cried over that.
changeReason: New restructures to emphasize the contrast between his available meal and inability to provide for Satoko.

TextENGNew: No... come to think of it, food wasn't the only issue...@kI was nowhere near capable of taking Satoko in by myself in the first place.
...Saving a single person... what an impossibly heavy thing.
changeReason: Old says 'such a heavy thing'; new says 'impossibly heavy thing'; adds emphasis matching Japanese.

TextENGNew: I'd watched TV and read manga where they always said things like,@k"I'll save my friends, I swear I'll save them"—and I'd always loved those rousing words.
changeReason: Old says 'comics'; new says 'manga'; Japanese 漫画 specifically means manga.

TextENGNew: ...Was that why I'd spouted those words so easily, swearing I would save her—because I wanted that rush of feeling good about myself...?
No... absolutely not.
...The reality was that I couldn't save her.
And I refused to accept the 'adult' way of thinking—that since we couldn't save her, there was nothing to do but stand by and watch.
changeReason: Old says 'didn't want to think'; new says 'refused to accept'; stronger stance.

TextENGNew: ...Had Satoko gone to school today...?
But I quickly realized that question didn't matter much.
...Whether she went or not... nothing about the situation she was trapped in would change.
If I couldn't save her, and nobody else could either...@k...then all we could do was pray for a miracle.
"............Are we... really so... powerless...?"
Clinging to the vague purpose of going to school... I sluggishly put on my shoes and stepped out the front door.
I'd only overslept by two or three hours, but the sunlight and the air felt completely different from the usual morning.
...Well, obviously.@kOnce it was past ten, you could hardly call it morning anymore.
...I didn't feel like taking the usual route to school.
I'd have to end up there eventually, but I couldn't bring myself to choose the shortest, most direct path—that's probably the best way to describe it.
...To put it more positively, I suppose I wanted time to walk alone and think.
changeReason: Old says 'didn't want to choose'; new says 'couldn't bring myself'; Japanese implies inability.

TextENGNew: I had to go to school.@k...Partly to confirm that Satoko was safe.
But... I still hadn't come up with anything.@k...Not a single thing since last night.
...So when I left the house, I headed in the opposite direction.
...Going this way, I'd pass by Rena's house... and then the dam construction site.
...It would be quite a detour.
I estimated how long the detour would take, and satisfied with the answer, started walking...
...Rena had brought me to the dam site a few times before.
One corner of the site had become a dumping ground for illegally discarded junk, which was perfect for Rena's beloved treasure hunting.
...Without that hobby, she'd pass for a perfectly normal girl.@k(...There were a few other things she could do without too, I supposed... but whatever...)
changeReason: New uses 'treasure hunting' matching Japanese ガラクタ漁り; old says 'fishing for junk.'

TextENGNew: The view suddenly opened up wide, and a strong gust of wind hit me.@k...There was nothing to block it... at this vast, open dam site.
...Maybe it was a good thing I came here, I thought.
...At the very least, thinking things over in a place like this was healthier than brooding in my cramped little room.
I took a deep breath and filled my lungs with the thick, cool air unique to Hinamizawa.
changeReason: Old says 'rich, cold'; new says 'thick, cool'; Japanese 濃い means thick/dense.

TextENGNew: ...Ding-a-ling, ding.
I spun around reflexively. It was a bicycle bell.
...Given where I was standing, it wasn't rung to get me out of the way.@k...Whoever it was had rung it to get my attention.
"Hello there, Keiichi-kun.@k@vS03/08/140800001_1. Didn't expect to run into you here."
changeReason: Old says 'Good evening'; new says 'Hello there'; Japanese こんにちは means hello, a time-of-day correction.

TextENGNew: Tomitake-san raised his hand in an easygoing gesture, flashing a friendly smile.
"Could you tell me how to get to the Furude Shrine from here?@k@vS03/08/140800003. I think I left my map back at the inn.@k@vS03/08/140800004. I've been kind of lost!"
changeReason: Old says 'I don't know where to go!'; new says 'I've been kind of lost!'; different characterization.

TextENGNew: I understood the spatial layout in my head, but explaining the route in words was tricky.
I was a bit annoyed at being asked something so troublesome... but when I realized it gave me a valid excuse for my detour from school, that feeling quickly faded.
"I could try to explain, but... it's a little hard to describe.@k@vS03/01/140100250. ...If you don't mind, I could just take you there."
"Really?!@k@vS03/08/140800006. That'd be a huge help.@k@vS03/08/140800007. Thank you!"
"...........But are you sure?@k@vS03/08/140800009. Don't you have somewhere to be?"
Tomitake-san seemed to have finally realized how odd it was for a student like me to be out here at this hour.
I breezily told him not to worry about it, turned around, and started walking toward the Furude Shrine.
Tomitake-san hastily wheeled his bike around and followed after me.
"Ah-ha-ha, sorry, sorry!@k@vS03/08/140800011. Don't sweat it—every man has times when he needs to break away from the pack and go lone wolf for a while."
...As expected of a freewheeling freelance photographer.@kIt was easier on me that he interpreted it that way.
"Man, I'm glad the weather's so nice today.@k@vS03/08/140800013. The forecast said a cold front was moving in, but it looks like it hasn't hit yet."
"Yeah.@k@vS03/01/140100252. But it doesn't seem like the rainy season's over yet, either."
"Hmm, I was planning a longer trip tomorrow, but...@k@vS03/08/140800015. Maybe I should rethink my plans a bit."
changeReason: Old says 'go out'; new says 'a longer trip'; Japanese 遠出 means a distant outing.

TextENGNew: Along the way, Tomitake-san launched into an unsolicited lecture about how precious Hinamizawa's nature was and what a treasure trove of rare wild birds it contained.
...I had no interest in the subject matter, but he seemed to be enjoying himself, so I decided not to interrupt.
"If you go up these stone steps, you'll reach the shrine grounds."
"Thank you!@k@vS03/08/140800017. You really saved me!@k@vS03/08/140800018. .........Hmm... I think I'm a little late."
Muttering to himself, Tomitake-san parked his bicycle to the side and began climbing the stone steps at a brisk pace.
...I didn't have a watch, but judging by my internal clock, it was still a little before noon.
changeReason: Old says 'late in the morning'; new says 'a little before noon'; Japanese お昼少し前 means shortly before noon.

TextENGNew: ...School wasn't far from here.
...Maybe I could afford to kill a little more time.
With that thought, I followed Tomitake-san up the stone steps.
At the top of the long staircase... the shrine grounds spread out before me, far too grand for a village this small.
...Tomitake-san was... oh, there he was.
...It looked like he'd been meeting someone here.
He'd gotten lost and arrived late, apparently.@k...He was bowing apologetically to a woman who seemed to be the person he was meeting.
That woman... our eyes met.
changeReason: New adds 'apologetically' for Japanese ぺこぺこ (bowing apologetically).

TextENGNew: "..................Oh my. Isn't that Maebara-kun?@k@vS03/09/140900002. Good afternoon.@k@vS03/09/140900003. No school today?"
"Huh?@k@vS03/08/140800020. Oh, Keiichi-kun, you followed me...?!"
"N-No, I wasn't really following you.@k@vS03/01/140100255. ...I just wanted to kill some time."
"Oh my.@k@vS03/09/140900005. ...Strolling in late like an executive—how elegant. A celebrity like you certainly knows how to make an entrance."
...Was I a celebrity now??
...Come to think of it, this woman had called me by name right away, too.
...I was mildly curious about what exactly she meant by 'famous.'
"Oh, you still can't remember my name?@k@vS03/09/140900007. ...Hee hee."

"This is Miyo Takano-san.@k@vS03/08/140800022. She works at the village clinic.@k@vS03/08/140800023. You seem like the healthy type, Keiichi-kun, so you probably haven't had much reason to visit, have you?"
"Hee hee...@k@vS03/09/140900009. Now that we've been properly introduced, I'll have to give you a little special treatment the next time you come in sick.@k@vS03/09/140900010. ...Hee hee!"
This woman who called herself Takano-san... I didn't really remember her.
...We might have passed each other on the street a few times, but I was fairly sure this was the first time we'd actually spoken.
Well, this was Hinamizawa—it wasn't unusual for someone I didn't know to know all about me.
...I glanced at Takano-san and noticed she was carrying a camera along with her bag.
"...Could it be that you two are photography buddies?@k@vS03/01/140100257. Ah, is that why you were meeting up?"
"Partners? Heavens, no.@k@vS03/09/140900012. I'm just an amateur, and Jirou-san is kind enough to show me the ropes.@k@vS03/09/140900013. Isn't that right...?"

"Mm... ah-ha-ha-ha...!@k@vS03/08/140800025. Th-That's not true at all!@k@vS03/08/140800026. Takano-san picks things up so quickly—she could take wonderful photos without any guidance from me!@k@vS03/08/140800027. Honestly.@k@vS03/08/140800028. Ah-ha-ha-ha...!"
...I couldn't tell which one of them was leading the other around.
...Well, they were certainly an entertaining couple to watch.
"The Watanagashi festival is finally the day after tomorrow.@k@vS03/09/140900015. I hope I can get some good photos this year."
"I'm planning to start shooting from the setup tomorrow.@k@vS03/08/140800030. I want to capture the festival's energy even in the preparation scenes."
Oh... that's right.@kThat village festival they called Watanagashi was the day after tomorrow, this Sunday...
"They pile up old futons and hold a memorial service to give thanks to Hinamizawa's guardian deity, Oyashiro-sama... something like that?"

"My, correct.@k@vS03/09/140900017. ...You're quite knowledgeable for someone who just moved here, Maebara-kun.@k@vS03/09/140900018. ...Yes, a festival... to give thanks to Oyashiro-sama.@k@vS03/09/140900019. .........Heh."
Takano-san smiled with a strangely knowing air, but she didn't explain what part of 'a festival to give thanks to Oyashiro-sama' warranted that laugh.
"...Good grief... Takano-san."
"...But I wonder what will happen this year."

"What happens twice will happen a third time, they say.@k@vS03/09/140900021. And it's already happened four times.@k@vS03/09/140900022. I'd say there's less basis for claiming it won't happen a fifth time, wouldn't you...?"
I understood the meaning behind their hushed exchange.
...On the day of the Watanagashi festival,@ka bizarre death—what people called 'Oyashiro-sama's curse'—always occurred, along with a disappearance they called being 'spirited away.'
These strange events had continued for four years running... and this year, the fifth, the festival was now just two days away...
"Oyashiro-sama, who brings divine wrath upon the village's enemies, huh?@k@vS03/08/140800034. ...If it does happen this year... I wonder who will be on the receiving end."
"...Oh, but I always make sure to visit the shrine and leave an offering whenever I come here!@k@vS03/08/140800036. It definitely won't be me."

"...Oh, is that so?@k@vS03/09/140900024. I hear Oyashiro-sama has been especially harsh on outsiders in recent years.@k@vS03/09/140900025. Maebara-kun may have only just moved here, but he's a proper resident of Hinamizawa..."
"You, Jirou-san, are just an outsider who drops by once a year.@k@vS03/09/140900027. ...I wonder... will he let you off the hook this year?"
"Th-That's harsh! Ah-ha-ha-ha-ha...!"
Tomitake-san was the only one with a strained laugh, but we all smiled, myself included.
...Oyashiro-sama—the one who had cursed and killed people connected to the dam construction project, one after another.
He killed the dam project foreman,@kkilled Satoko's parents for supporting the dam...@kand then, two years later, killed even her aunt.
If being spirited away was also part of the curse, then Satoshi was another victim.
...When I thought about it that way,@kthe Houjou name dominated an overwhelming proportion of the serial mysterious deaths.
Half of all the deaths and disappearances involved members of the Houjou family.
...The Houjous were villagers of Hinamizawa.
...Did that mean Oyashiro-sama punished—cursed—them with particular severity because they were villagers who had dared to support the dam?
"...............Have you heard that Satoko's uncle has come back?"
Takano-san, who had been chatting happily with Tomitake-san, was caught off guard by my sudden question and blinked in surprise.
"Oh, I'm sorry.@k@vS03/09/140900029. What was that?"
"Well... Satoko's parents were killed by the curse, and her aunt was killed by the curse too.@k@vS03/01/140100261. ...So I was thinking, going in order... maybe her uncle would be next."

"...Last year's curse killed her aunt in a horrible way... and the uncle got scared and fled to town, right?@k@vS03/01/140100263. ...Oyashiro-sama doesn't forgive people who try to abandon the village and run... does he...?"
...I hadn't meant to be this insistent.
It was just that... once I opened my mouth, the words kept pouring out on their own.
...That's right.@kOyashiro-sama's curse.
...Of course I didn't believe in something as irrational as a curse... but it was a fact that every year, misfortune befell the village's enemies in the form of accidents and incidents.
Just as Takano-san had said... it had already happened four times.@kNo one could guarantee there wouldn't be a fifth.

"..........Hmm.@k@vS03/09/140900031. ...That's an interesting theory.@k@vS03/09/140900032. It's certainly true that the Houjou name appears disproportionately among past victims.@k@vS03/09/140900033. ...Following that pattern, the possibility of her uncle dying or disappearing this year... can't be dismissed.@k@vS03/09/140900034. ...Hee hee hee."
"...Come now, Takano-san.@k@vS03/08/140800039. You shouldn't laugh about misfortune that might actually happen."
"Ufufufu, I'm sorry.@k@vS03/09/140900036. But Oyashiro-sama's curse is my life's work, after all.@k@vS03/09/140900037. Hee hee hee..."
Takano-san carried a somewhat intellectual, mysterious aura about her... and her life's work was every bit as enigmatic.
...She probably had a real fascination with inexplicable supernatural phenomena.

"...You seem very well-versed in Oyashiro-sama's curse, Takano-san.@k@vS03/01/140100265. ...What do you think?@k@vS03/01/140100266. ...Given that he's a Houjou who's come back, what are the chances the uncle gets cursed...?"
"...My, my.@k@vS03/09/140900039. ...You seem awfully eager to see this uncle of hers dead.@k@vS03/09/140900040. Hee hee."
"...Th-... that's not what I meant..."
"Ah, ahahahahaha!@k@vS03/08/140800041. Hmm... who knows?@k@vS03/08/140800042. ...It all depends on the whims of the divine..."
"...Maebara-kun.@k@vS03/09/140900042. Do you know who Santa Claus really is?"
Huh?@kCaught off guard by the sudden, seemingly random question, I couldn't answer right away.
"What, you don't know?@k@vS03/09/140900044. .........It's Daddy.@k@vS03/09/140900045. Every family's daddy."

"...W-What?@k@vS03/01/140100269. ...That's what you meant?@k@vS03/01/140100270. Well, obviously.@k@vS03/01/140100271. Behind everything, there's ultimately a human being.@k@vS03/01/140100272. After all, this is a world with nothing in it but humans.@k@vS03/01/140100273. Every phenomenon should be explainable in human terms."
It came out a bit awkwardly, but Takano-san didn't seem at all displeased with my answer.

"Oh... to realize that at your age—impressive. You're exactly right.@k@vS03/09/140900047. Everything that happens in the world of humans is caused by humans, for human reasons.@k@vS03/09/140900048. ...Well then, Maebara-kun, let me ask you once more."
"This time, not about Santa Claus... but about Oyashiro-sama."
"...What is the true identity... of Oyashiro-sama's curse?"
Takano-san... took a step closer, peering into my eyes... as she posed the question.

...If Santa Claus was really children's parents in every household,@kand if the legend was perpetuated by corporations trying to profit off the Christmas shopping season,@k...then what was the true nature of Oyashiro-sama's curse?

"Oh... but didn't you just say it yourself?@k@vS03/09/140900052. That this is a world with only humans in it.@k@vS03/09/140900053. That every phenomenon can be explained in human terms.@k@vS03/09/140900054. ...Hee hee."

"......Keiichi-kun.@k@vS03/08/140800044. This stays between us, all right?@k@vS03/08/140800045. ...Takano-san believes that the string of mysterious deaths attributed to Oyashiro-sama's curse...@k@vS03/08/140800046. ...may actually be premeditated murders carried out by the people of Hinamizawa, based on some kind of ritual."

"Don't misunderstand me.@k@vS03/09/140900056. My life's work is studying the doctrines, philosophies, and culture behind it from a folkloristic perspective.@k@vS03/09/140900057. I have absolutely no interest in identifying the culprit.@k@vS03/09/140900058. Please don't confuse the two."
...Um...... I could at least tell that she was saying something extraordinary.
...If her words were a meal, it felt like someone had crammed an entire platter's worth of food down a duck's throat—that's how overwhelmed I was.

"To truly understand it, you'd have to trace things all the way back to ancient times, when Hinamizawa was still known as Onigafuchi Village.@k@vS03/09/140900060. There were people there—half-human, half-demon—who believed they had mingled their blood with demons, and these so-called transcendents—"
Sensing she was now on her home turf, Takano-san leaned forward eagerly and began to unfold an outlandish narrative.
...When Tomitake-san noticed I was being overwhelmed, he stepped in to rescue me.
"Takano-san, Takano-san.@k@vS03/08/140800048. You can't just spring all of that on Keiichi-kun out of nowhere...@k@vS03/08/140800049. Look, the poor kid's dumbfounded."

"..................Oh my... is he?@k@vS03/09/140900062. If you'd like to hear the rest, just ask me anytime.@k@vS03/09/140900063. I have heaps of the sort of gruesome tales that boys your age tend to love."
"...Th-Thank you.@k@vS03/01/140100276. If I ever feel like it..."
I politely and diplomatically declined for the time being...
Takano-san seemed a little miffed that Tomitake-san had dampened her enthusiasm, but she let the topic drop for now.
Even so... it had genuinely been an interesting conversation.
...Oyashiro-sama's curse, which I'd assumed was some supernatural phenomenon...@k...was actually murder... committed by people of the village.

"Well, I mean... thinking about it logically, that has to be the case.@k@vS03/01/140100278. ...People don't just die from curses.@k@vS03/01/140100279. ...Someone has to be killing them and disguising it as one."
"Indeed.@k@vS03/09/140900065. ...As for who that someone is... hee hee, perhaps it's safer not to look into it."
"......You seem like you already know, Takano-san.@k@vS03/01/140100281. ...Maybe not with proof... but at least with conviction."
"Oh... whatever gave you that impression?"
It was just a vague feeling.
...I simply couldn't imagine that this woman's feverish passion for research would be tempered by something as tame as 'it's taboo' or 'it's safer not to.'
She was the kind of person... who thrived on walking the very edge of what was forbidden—the precipice of risk and taboo.

"That's enough, Keiichi-kun.@k@vS03/08/140800051. ...I just rescued you from that, and now you're going to set her off about Oyashiro-sama's curse again?@k@vS03/08/140800052. ...Ahh... I was hoping to take a nice stroll while it was still cool..."
It seemed Tomitake-san was a little disappointed—my bringing up a strange topic had derailed the outing he'd planned.
...But right now... there was something I wanted to ask, just briefly.
...whether Satoko's uncle... would be chosen as this year's target of the curse.
...If he were................@k...then last year's miracle would repeat itself.
...Another terrible incident would occur... but Satoko would once again be freed from her wretched circumstances.

"..........Well.@k@vS03/09/140900068. I do have my suspicions, of course—a rough idea of the sort of people who are probably involved.@k@vS03/09/140900069. It's the natural, inevitable conclusion you arrive at when you study the history of Onigafuchi Village..."
"...Who... are you talking about?"
...What would I even do with that knowledge?
...I posed the question to the other me lurking inside... but there was no answer.
"My, how eager.@k@vS03/09/140900071. ...And what would you do with that information?"
"......Nothing in particular."
"...If you found out, you might put yourself in danger, you know?@k@vS03/09/140900073. Maybe the fifth year's curse would fall on you...@k@vS03/09/140900074. ...Hee hee."
Takano-san, toying with taboo, smiled with an impish satisfaction... as though nothing in the world could delight her more.
...She was probably just trying to tease me, but I had no intention of playing along.
"...Hmm.@k@vS03/01/140100285. ...You're putting on airs with all this grand talk... but it sounds like you don't really know either, do you, Takano-san?"
A flicker of irritation at Takano-san's roundabout way of speaking—@kand before I knew it, that emotion had slipped past my lips... like water pouring through a sieve.
...I was startled by how raw and direct my outburst had been.@k...And of course, I wasn't the only one taken aback.

"...............Oh my.@k@vS03/09/140900076. ...Quite the tongue on you.@k@vS03/09/140900077. For a mere boy, you can even be provocative?@k@vS03/09/140900078. How unexpectedly wild.@k@vS03/09/140900079. .........I don't dislike that sort of thing, you know."
Takano-san's tongue briefly poked out... and I was convinced it had to be long, thin, and forked at the tip like a serpent's.
"Very well, then.@k@vS03/09/140900081. ...I suppose I could tell you."

"...But before I do, you'll swear to three things.@k@vS03/09/140900083. ...No matter what I tell you, you won't regret it.@k@vS03/09/140900084. You won't share it with anyone.@k@vS03/09/140900085. And you will never reveal that I was the one who told you."
I swallowed hard... and nodded.
Tomitake-san, with an 'oh well' sort of expression, gave a wry, vague smile and lit a cigarette.
"Please tell me.@k@vS03/01/140100288. ...Who is carrying out the curse?@k@vS03/01/140100289. Who decides... who gets cursed?!"
...It was only after those words left my mouth... that I finally understood why I was so desperate to know...
